issue_title: Should percent encode the query key and value when being encoded as str
issue_body:
I use furl(v1.1) in http proxy app,  and receive url in server side(the http proxy received from client):

```
>>> url_received = 'http://example.com/?redirect=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.example.com%2F'
>>> parsed = furl.furl(url_received)
```

After do something with the parsed url, and generate url in client side(to be sent to the real server):

```
>>> url_to_be_sent = parsed.url 
>>> print(url_to_be_sent)
'http://example.com/?redirect=http://www.example.com/'
>>> print(url_to_be_sent.query)
'redirect=http://www.example.com/'
```

The "redirect=http://www.example.com/" part should be percent encoded as original query "redirect=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.example.com%2F"

issue_title: 'Del' and 'Add' colors don't display correctly when converted to HTML.
issue_body:
cdiff outputs block diffed text with red foreground AND red background, or green foreground AND green background, but I think the terminal is kind enough to display it with just the background color set. I noticed this when I was converting output from `cdiff` to html using `aha`. For example (I've trimmed a bit of the unnecessary bits): $ diff <(echo 'a') <(echo 'b') --unified | cdiff -s --color=always | aha stdin 
    1 a                                                                                1 b
I think it's because aha is converting from ESC[0mESC[33m1ESC[0m ESC[31mESC[7mESC[31maESC[0mESC[31mESC[0m ESC[0mESC[33m1ESC[0m ESC[32mESC[7mESC[32mbESC[0mESC[32mESC[0m which you'll notice has `ESC[31mESC[7mESC[31ma`, which I think should just be `ESC[31mESC[7ma`. As it is, it sets the foreground, reverses, and then sets the foreground again.

issue_title: add_metaclass fails with name-mangled slots
issue_body:
The `add_metalclass` decorator assumes that [all members of `__slots__` will be exposed in the class `__dict__`](https://github.com/benjaminp/six/blob/63b2661b94463b59752396a3562c9e5ebe672f97/six.py#L844), but this isn't true for name-mangled slots (those having at least two leading underscores and at most one trailing underscore). Example:

```python
import six

class Meta(type):
    pass

@six.add_metaclass(Meta)
class Foo(object):
    __slots__ = (
        'something',
        '_something',
        '__something'
    )
```

Running this gives the error:

```
$ python bug.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "bug.py", line 7, in <module>
    class Foo(object):
  File "/home/apearce/tmp/six-bug/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/six.py", line 844, in wrapper
    orig_vars.pop(slots_var)
KeyError: '__something'
```

This is because the `__something` slot is given the key `_Foo__something` key in `__dict__`, not `__something`.

## Workarounds

Using `with_metaclass` works.

```python
import six

class Meta(type):
    pass

class Some(six.with_metaclass(Meta, object)):
    __slots__ = (
        'something',
        '_something',
        '__something'
    )
```

## Solutions

One way around this would be to detect if name mangling will happen.

```python
def _mangled_name(cls, name):
    """Return the mangled version of name belonging to cls, if appropriate.

    Python applies name mangling if the name starts with at least two
    underscores and ends with at most one underscore.
    """
    mangled = name.startswith('__') and not name.endswith('__')
    if mangled:
        name = '_{0}{1}'.format(cls.__name__, name)
    return name


def add_metaclass(metaclass):
    """Class decorator for creating a class with a metaclass."""
    def wrapper(cls):
        orig_vars = cls.__dict__.copy()
        slots = orig_vars.get('__slots__')
        if slots is not None:
            if isinstance(slots, str):
                slots = [slots]
            for slots_var in slots:
                orig_vars.pop(_mangled_name(cls, slots_var))
        orig_vars.pop('__dict__', None)
        orig_vars.pop('__weakref__', None)
        return metaclass(cls.__name__, cls.__bases__, orig_vars)
    return wrapper
```

I'd open an MR with this, but am not sure if this is the ideal solution.

issue_title: DotMap fails for recursive structures
issue_body:
Setting a child value to the parent DotMap will lead to infinite recursions.
Especially this approach works fine for normal `dict`s.

```python
> some_dict = dict()
> some_dict['some_dict'] = some_dict
> print(some_dict)
{'some_dict': {...}}
```

```python
> from dotmap import DotMap
> some_dotmap = DotMap()
> some_dotmap.some_dotmap = some_dotmap
> print(some_dotmap)
> print(some_dotmap.toDict())

…
RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded
```

Best regards, Christian

issue_title: Add support for custom fields
issue_body:
- Go to zendesk and add a custom field in your ticket (admin > ticket fields > add custom field)
- Add a custom field and copy-paste the ID
- If you now go to the ticket view you will be able to see new fields on the left-hand side of the ticket.

You can now fill this custom field at Ticket creation by doing this API call:

```bash
curl https://[SUBDOMAIN].zendesk.com/api/v2/tickets.json -d '{"ticket": { "custom_fields": [{"id": [CUSTOM FIELD ID], "value": "HELLO"}], "requester": {"name": "John Doe", "email": "john@domain.com"}, "subject": "This is the subject!", "comment": { "body": "This is the question." }}}' -H "Content-Type: application/json" -v -u [EMAIL ADDRESS]/token:[TOKEN] -X POST
```

It would be great if zenpy supported this, perhaps as:

```python
Ticket(custom_fields=[CustomField(id=1234, value="Hello", CustomField(id=1235, value=15), CustomField(id=1236, value=datetime.now()], ...)
```

Custom fields can be strings, numbers or dates.

It does seem that it takes a while for custom fields to become "active" in the API. I.e. there is some delay between creating a custom field and actually being able to POST it via the API...

What is the proper way to close connection in REUSABLE strategy
issue_body:
For sync strategy `connection.unbind()` seems is enough.
But for reusable strategy we need to care about created threads under the hood and etc. So seems only `unbinding` isn't enough.

I am using this approach right now:
```
for worker in connection.strategy.pool.workers:
    worker.connection.unbind()
connection.unbind()
connection.strategy.pool.terminate_pool()
```

Any ideas about proper way? I need to prevent collisions on the low level with reintialization connection with new settings (host for examle)

issue_title: Fallback scenario is ugly
issue_body:
In #117 and specifically in https://github.com/jaraco/keyring/issues/117#issuecomment-171330756, the assertion was made that

> @rsyring: Getting a None returned is probably never going to be the actual desired behavior.

But as reported in [bitbucket issue migration 60](https://github.com/jeffwidman/bitbucket-issue-migration/issues/60#issuecomment-174611323), that's not the case for libraries that optionally include keyring support but fallback to prompting for a password when none is found in the keyring ([bitbucket_issue_migration](https://github.com/jeffwidman/bitbucket-issue-migration/blob/master/migrate.py#L103-L104), ...) and almost certainly others.

I'm thinking we should change the fail backend to only error on set/delete.

issue_title: default backend sort order is not deterministic
issue_body:
the default backend is chosen using the following code:


```
#!python

    # if the user doesn't specify a keyring, we apply a default one
    if keyring is None:

        keyrings = backend.get_all_keyring()
        # rank according to the supported result
        keyrings.sort(key = lambda x: -x.supported())
        # get the most recommended one
        keyring = keyrings[0]
```

this isn't deterministic enough because subsequent runs may return a different value if <backend>.supported() is the same value for multiple backends.

for example, on ubuntu, if i run the following a bunch of times:

```
#!python

import keyring
print keyring.get_keyring()
```

sometimes i'll get keyring.backends.Gnome.Keyring and sometimes i'll get keyring.backends.file.PlaintextKeyring.

issue_title: JWT strings different between python 2 and 3
issue_body:
Because python dictionaries arent ordered by default in 2 and are ordered by default in 3, pyjwt generates different jwt strings because of https://github.com/jpadilla/pyjwt/blob/master/jwt/api_jws.py#L83. This affects applications and libs that are doing jwt comparisons.

Would it be acceptable to change that to use an OrderedDict instead? That way, the jwt strings will be the same between 2 and 3.

issue_title: iter_parse method
issue_body:
Acceptance Criteria
--------------------------

A method, ``term.iter_parse()``, that can be used in the following manner:
```
>>> for part in Terminal().iter_parse('\x1b[10Cblahblah\x1b[0m'):
...    print((ucs, ucs.is_sequence, ucs.capname, ucs.params))
...
('\x1b[10C', True, 'cuf', ('10',))
('blahblah', False, None, None))
('\x1b[0m', True, 'sgr', ('0',))
```

There are several places in the codebase where sequences are "iterated", these sections of code should be replaced by the new ``iter_parse(term, ucs)`` function added to blessings/sequences.py, and proxying stub method added to the Terminal class.

Details
----------

One thing blessed does right now is simply emit or strip sequences, but it doesn't "comprehend" them in the way curtsies might be able to. 

The value yielded should be a namedtuple() instance, values ``.ucs(str), .is_sequence(bool), .capname(str), .params(tuple(str))``

This can be achieved by modifying the regex parse builders in blessed/sequences.py to include ``(?P<name>...)`` in the expressions compiled.

issue_title: Support opening files from an in-memory buffer

I have a project which wants to operate directly on FITS files which are already in memory, residing in a Python string/bytes object. It would be very beneficial if the fitsio Python module supported this mode of operation.

As it is, I am forced to write a temporary file out to disk, then re-read it from disk using `fitsio.read()`. This just adds overhead that I don't want. It also forces me to handle cleaning up these temporary files if something goes wrong.

The cfitsio library has `fits_open_memfile()`, which could be used to handle this situation.

Is it possible to add support for this use case?

issue_title: Merge "multi" methods with "simple" methods.
issue_body:
The code in the simple methods like `puresnmp.get` predate the functions in `puresnmp.multiget`. Other, similar cases exist for `walk` and `set`.

There's quite some code-duplication. It is possible to keep the "multi" functions, and make the "simple" functions thin wrappers around those "multi" methods. This should remove the duplication and still keep both versions available.

Why keep both, and why not *only* keep the "multi" variants?

One main aim of the library is to keep the API as simple as possible. Having both versions makes:

* The difference between both calls explicit
* the API of the "simple" methods as simple as possible.

issue_title: 'twist web -p onion:80' doesn't always log the allocated .onion address
issue_body:
Running `twist web -p onion:80 --path .` is a quick way to expose a directory to a newly-allocated .onion address. When you run this, txtorcon always launches a new Tor instance, and when the descriptor is published, it logs the new `.onion` address to twistd.log, so you can tell where to connect:

```
$ twist web -p onion:18832 --path src
...
2017-08-15T22:48:43-0700 [TorControlProtocol,client] 100% At least one descriptor uploaded.
2017-08-15T22:48:43-0700 [TorControlProtocol,client] Started hidden service port 18832
2017-08-15T22:48:43-0700 [TorControlProtocol,client]   listening on ewrfksney6ycxnhx.onion.onion
```

(the double `.onion.onion` is weird, and should be fixed, but that's not the point of this bug)

However, if you want to use a system Tor (e.g. on a linux box where Tor is already running), then you use something like `-p "onion:80:controlPort=unix\:/var/run/tor/control"`. And in *that* case, the final `listening on` line is never printed, so there's no easy way to discover what address has been allocated:

```
$ twist web -p "onion:18832:controlPort=unix\:/var/run/tor/control" --path src
2017-08-15T22:50:42-0700 [-] Will delete "/tmp/tortmpD2k0_W" at shutdown.
2017-08-15T22:50:42-0700 [twisted.application.runner._runner.Runner#info] Starting reactor...
2017-08-15T22:50:43-0700 [TorControlProtocol,client] Site starting on 44173
2017-08-15T22:50:43-0700 [twisted.web.server.Site#info] Starting factory <twisted.web.server.Site instance at 0x7fe14f5ba368>
2017-08-15T22:50:43-0700 [TorControlProtocol,client] 100% Waiting for descriptor upload...
2017-08-15T22:51:18-0700 [TorControlProtocol,client] 100% At least one descriptor uploaded.
2017-08-15T22:51:18-0700 [TorControlProtocol,client] Started hidden service port 18832
```

I'm pretty sure I managed to trigger this case without appending the `controlPort=` clause (since I didn't remember that was even possible until meejah just reminded me of it), but I don't know what I might have done to cause it.

Maybe the print-the-address callback is only being attached to the Deferred chain in one of the (many) ways to launch a Tor daemon.

issue_title: BrokerConnection blocks forever while receiving
issue_body:
I have a slightly older version of `kafka-python`, but the code in master seems like it should still have this problem.

https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/blob/master/kafka/conn.py#L811

    def _recv(self):
        responses = []
        SOCK_CHUNK_BYTES = 4096
        while True:
            try:
                if self._sock.gettimeout() is None:
                    raise Exception('This operation will never succeed')
                data = self._sock.recv(SOCK_CHUNK_BYTES)
                # We expect socket.recv to raise an exception if there is not
                # enough data to read the full bytes_to_read
                # but if the socket is disconnected, we will get empty data
                # without an exception raised
                if not data:
                    ...

And this exception actually gets thrown in my setup.  Obviously, if you are trying to receive on blocking socket a number of bytes less than Kafka actually sent, this will not work.  In my case, what seems to be happening is Kafka gives up after its own timeout, and this receive errors and breaks from the loop.

I see there a lot of code around this code that flips timeout / blocking flags on this socket and it feels like this is some sort of race condition, but I didn't dig deeper.

issue_title: Exclude files from listing (and directory tarballs)
issue_body:
Related to #13

### Requirements:
* Implement **--exclude** command argument using POSIX shell patterns.
* Support multiple **--exclude** definitions.
* Update **--plugin** to use multiple definitions too, for consistency (while maintaining retrocompatibility).
* Esclusion function at **app.config['exclude']** (better customizability when used as library). Value **None** must be also valid.
* If exclusion function returns **True** for a given path (relative to base directory), it will be ignored in both listing and tarballs.

issue_title: [XServer] Library makes program hard crash when monitor is out of bounds
issue_body:
General information:

* OS name: _Ubuntu (`ubuntu-desktop`)_
* OS version: __18.04 LTS__
* OS architecture: _64 bits_
* Resolutions:
    * Monitor 1: _1920x1080 (laptop built-in)_
    * Monitor 2: _1366x768 (external)_
* Python version: _3.6.5_
* MSS version: __3.2.0__


For GNU/Linux users:

* Display server protocol and version, if known: __X server__ _(X.Org version: 1.19.6)_
* Desktop Environment: _Gnome 3_
* Composite Window Manager name and version: __???__ (The default one, I guess?)


### Description of the warning/error
This error occurs when the a monitor that is out of bounds is specified in the `grab()` call.
```python
from mss import mss

screenshotter = mss()
monitor = {
    "left": -30,
    "top": 0,
    "width": 100,
    "height": 100,
}
try:
    screenshotter.grab(monitor)  # X Error
except BaseException as e:
    # Never reached
    print("Handling error")
```
Using a monitor like `{"left": 0, "top": 0, "width": 10000, "height": 100}` has the same result.
### Full message
```stderr
X Error of failed request:  BadMatch (invalid parameter attributes)
  Major opcode of failed request:  73 (X_GetImage)
  Serial number of failed request:  91
  Current serial number in output stream:  91
```
The message is printed to `stderr`, then the Python interpreter quits with exit code `1`.

### Other details
I discovered this because I let my program generate a box based on the location of the cursor. Then I take the screenshot of only the cursor area.

While it might be better for efficiency not to check the bounds of the monitor, I personally don't think causing this type of hard crash is acceptable. It's up to you. If you decide not to check bounds, I think a mention of this in the documentation would be appropriate.

Hi 
I am working with sanic-jwt and marshmallow to make the requests data validation.

If data validation fails I am calling the abort method from sanic with the errors. This worked as intended but after I added the protected decorator to the method the result changed. 
Now I am receiving this response even when the endpoint is receiving a valid Authorization token.
```

    "reasons": {
        "polygon": [
            "Missing data for required field."

}
```
The reasons are the errors that I called the abort method with. 
If the validation is successful I dont get any error. 

One thing that worked to not get the InvalidAuthorizationHeader was to return a response with the authorization header instead of the abort methor.

issue_title: Certificate verification when connecting over HTTPS
issue_body:
When I try to connect to CouchDB over an HTTPS connection, urllib3 complains that it is making an insecure request:

> InsecureRequestWarning: Unverified HTTPS request is being made. Adding certificate verification is strongly advised. See: https://urllib3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/security.html

Unfortunately, the advise given on the above link can't be applied because pycouchdb doesn't expose any interface with Requests or urllib3.

I tried to get around this by patching the urllib3 connection pool classes (per [this answer on StackOverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/a/22253656/876937)):

```python
def patch_connection_pools(**constructor_kwargs):
    """
    Override the default parameters of the HTTPConnectionPool and
    HTTPSConnectionPool constructors
    """
    from requests.packages.urllib3 import connectionpool, poolmanager

    def subtype_connection_pool(base):
        class _ConnectionPool(base):
            def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
                kwargs.update(constructor_kwargs)
                super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)

        return _ConnectionPool

    poolmanager.pool_classes_by_scheme['http'] = subtype_connection_pool(connectionpool.HTTPConnectionPool)
    poolmanager.pool_classes_by_scheme['https'] = subtype_connection_pool(connectionpool.HTTPSConnectionPool)

patch_connection_pools(maxsize=16, cert_reqs='CERT_REQUIRED', ca_certs='/path/to/my/cacert.pem')
```

The patching is definitely being called by pycouchdb (verified by putting a `print` in the patched constructor), but the `cert_reqs` and `ca_certs` arguments have no effect on the warning given by urllib3. (I only assume `maxsize` is being respected.)

I haven't delved through the source of these libraries too deeply, so I might be targeting the wrong place. Is there any other way I can get pycouchdb to do certificate verification?

issue_title: Node-formatting ignored when using Unidic unless -O is set to empty string
issue_body:
As reported by @massongit in pull request #98 , node-formatting seems to be ignored by mecab when using Unidic. Please refer to [taku910/mecab#41](https://github.com/taku910/mecab/issues/41).

A workaround is to force natto-py to accept an empty string value for output `-O`.

Steps to reproduce:

1. Install Unidic 2.1.2
2. Execute code snippet A below to observe that natto-py will not be able to respect the node-formatting specified, but instead use the default `node-format` for Unidic
3. Contrast code snippet A (`natto-py`)with B and C (using `mecab` from command-line)

```
# Snippet A
# Note that node-formatting is ignored and defaults to node-format-unidic
>>> with MeCab(r'-d /opt/mecab/lib/mecab/dic/unidic -F%m\t%t,%f[12]\n') as nm:
...     for n in nm.parse('日本語だよ、これが。', as_nodes=True):
...         print(n.feature)
...
日本    ニッポン        ニッポン        日本    名詞-固有名詞-地名-国
語      ゴ      ゴ      語      名詞-普通名詞-一般
だ      ダ      ダ      だ      助動詞  助動詞-ダ       終止形-一般
よ      ヨ      ヨ      よ      助詞-終助詞
、                      、      補助記号-読点
これ    コレ    コレ    此れ    代名詞
が      ガ      ガ      が      助詞-格助詞
。                      。      補助記号-句点
EOS
```

```
# Snippet B
# Note that node-formatting is ignored and defaults to node-format-unidic
$ echo '日本語だよ、これが。' | mecab -d /opt/mecab/lib/mecab/dic/unidic/ -F%m\\t%t,%f[12]\\n
日本    ニッポン        ニッポン        日本    名詞-固有名詞-地名-国
語      ゴ      ゴ      語      名詞-普通名詞-一般
だ      ダ      ダ      だ      助動詞  助動詞-ダ       終止形-一般
よ      ヨ      ヨ      よ      助詞-終助詞
、                      、      補助記号-読点
これ    コレ    コレ    此れ    代名詞
が      ガ      ガ      が      助詞-格助詞
。                      。      補助記号-句点
EOS
```

```
# Snippet C
# node-formatting is honored when -O is passed an empty string!
$ echo '日本語だよ、これが。' | mecab -d /opt/mecab/lib/mecab/dic/unidic/ -F%m\\t%t,%f[12]\\n -O ""
日本    2,固

issue_title: Comment Height Bug With Collapsed Groups
issue_body:
When collapsing a group, the height of a cell comment gets entirely botched. If left to the default size, many will be a height of zero. If specifying a static size such as 200, it will not use the value and each subsequent comment height in the group will grow larger and larger.

``` python
import xlsxwriter
import subprocess

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook("test.xlsx");
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

# Example 1: Ten grouped items without comment height specified
worksheet.write(0, 0, "something")
worksheet.set_row(0, None, None, {'collapsed' : True})
for i in range(1,10):
    worksheet.write(i, 0, "sub something %i" %i)
    worksheet.set_row(i, None, None, {'hidden' : True, 'level' : 1})
    worksheet.write_comment(i, 0, "comment")

#Example 2: Ten grouped items with constant height of 200
worksheet.write(10, 0, "something else")
worksheet.set_row(10, None, None, {'collapsed' : True})
for i in range(11,20):
    worksheet.write(i, 0, "sub something else %i" %i)
    worksheet.set_row(i, None, None, {'hidden' : True, 'level' : 1})
    worksheet.write_comment(i, 0, "comment", {"height":200})

workbook.close()
subprocess.call(('cmd', '/C', 'start', '', "test.xlsx"))
```

issue_title: Allow vba_extract.py to support an output file name
issue_body:
I have created a small patch that allows the user to specify an output filename when running vba_extract.py. The primary reason for this is that if I extract the VBA from two (or more) Excel sheets, in the same directory the new extracted file overwrites the old one.

I proposed
The new syntax is would be:

    python vba_extract.py inputfile.xlsm outputVBA.bin

The other option would be to base the output filename on the inputfile name. For example

    python vba_extract.py inputfile.xlsm 

would generate vbaProject-inputfile.bin

The code change is pretty trivial either way but seems like it would be helpful. Let me know your thoughts.

issue_labels: [{'id': 23779648, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/jmcnamara/XlsxWriter/labels/bug', 'name': 'bug', 'color': 'fc2929', 'default': True}, {'id': 115685664, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/jmcnamara/XlsxWriter/labels/ready%20to%20close', 'name': 'ready to close', 'color': 'bfe5bf', 'default': False}, {'id': 191299581, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/jmcnamara/XlsxWriter/labels/short%20term', 'name': 'short term', 'color': '009800', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
If a XlsxWriter table contains non-unique headers it leads to a corrupt file. Excel requires unique, case-insensitive headers.

Sample program that demonstrates the issue:

``` python
import xlsxwriter 

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('test.xlsx')
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

worksheet.add_table(0, 0, 1, 1, {'columns': [{'header': 'ID'},
                                             {'header': 'ID'}]})
workbook.close()
```

issue_title: On demand import of external "extras"
issue_body:
First of all: Love the project! Many thanks for doing this.

I would like to suggest that the external extras, that are not really needed for the main functionality of PyFunctional, gets removed from the pip requirements.txt. It translates into a transitive dependency for all my projects using PyFunctional, and especially the lzma one fails to install on most Ubuntu systems (including the default docker one), making automated install of my projects quite problematic.

It should be easy to add the import lzma (or bz, whatever) in the functions where they are needed, rather than in the header of the files. Then these non-essential packages can be cut from requirements.txt, and people who wish to use the features could simply enable them by making the packages available.

issue_title: Batch inefficiency - requesting 5 nodes leads to 6 HTTP requests
issue_body:
I thought the batch support would be a great boon to performance, but it only helps for writes and indexing in the db. Here's an example:

    nodes = []
    with conn.transaction():
        for i in range(5):
            nodes.append(conn.node[i])

... while this looks good, and does in fact pull all the nodes in a batch, right after the transaction ends `client.Node`'s constructor is called 5 times, one for each new node. Unfortunately, the constructor calls the database again, which leads to more requests than if we hadn't batched in the first place. The requests look something like:

    POST http://localhost:7474/db/data/batch
    [[('to', u'/node/0'), ('method', 'GET'), ('id', 0)],
     [('to', u'/node/1'), ('method', 'GET'), ('id', 1)],
     [('to', u'/node/2'), ('method', 'GET'), ('id', 2)],
     [('to', u'/node/3'), ('method', 'GET'), ('id', 3)],
     [('to', u'/node/4'), ('method', 'GET'), ('id', 4)]]
    GET http://localhost:7474/db/data/node/0
    GET http://localhost:7474/db/data/node/1
    GET http://localhost:7474/db/data/node/2
    GET http://localhost:7474/db/data/node/3
    GET http://localhost:7474/db/data/node/4

(where the list below the POST is the attached  json body)

We've subclassed `Node` to avoid this particular behavior, but this sort of eager access to the database can be found throughout the client. Any sort of improvement would be really helpful.

To anyone else with this problem- I don't have time to submit a patch, but I can provide our workarounds if you need them.

issue_title: Tobit models
issue_body: I'm interested in the case where censoring can be on the left or the right (not both) for each observation. In my particular use case, all observations are censored on one or the other side. For example, with survival, say we check in on patients' status at intervals, and record only whether they were alive or dead when we check. When they are observed dead, we know only an upper bound on their survival time; when they are observed alive, we know only a lower bound. From what I understand, this can modeled using what  in the literature is called a Tobit model. It looks like someone was working on this for the statsmodels package, but may not have finished it.

issue_title: Public named URLs don't work with Django's script prefix
issue_body:
In Django 1.10, when using `django_setup(set_prefix=True)` and `FORCED_SCRIPT_PREFIX`, public named URLs do not work and login URLs lead to infinite redirects.

Stronghold during load time is `reverse()`ing all the URLs from `STRONGHOLD_PUBLIC_NAMED_URLS` and storing them as public URLs.  Then, in the middleware it compares them with `request.path_info` to determine if the current URL being requested is a public URL.

With Django 1.10, [a new parameter](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/releases/1.10/#urls) to `django.setup()` was introduced to make `FORCE_SCRIPT_PREFIX` applicable outside the requests. So, when `reverse()` is called outside a request such during the loading of the stronghold conf.py, now, it takes into account `FORCE_SCRIPT_PREFIX`. Also, `request.path_info` means path without the script prefix.  So, the reversed URLs contain script prefix and the compared value `request.path_info` does not have script prefix.  So, they never match and are not treated as public URLs.

issue_title: Support OSX
issue_body:
I think this is missing

```
if hasattr(fcntl, "F_FULLFSYNC"):
    fcntl.fcntl(f.fileno(), fcntl.F_FULLFSYNC)
else:
    os.fsync(f.fileno())
```

on OSX.

As mentioned here [0] and here [1] fsync doesn't wait for the changes to hit the disk there.

issue_title: TypeError when disconnecting
issue_body:
Hi Miguel,

I'm using the new version of Flask-SocketIO and when I connect and subsequently disconnect, I get this error after a short time:

    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/flask/app.py", line 1836, in __call__
        return self.wsgi_app(environ, start_response)
      File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/engineio/middleware.py", line 32, in __call__
        return self.engineio_app.handle_request(environ, start_response)
      File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/socketio/server.py", line 286, in handle_request
        return self.eio.handle_request(environ, start_response)
      File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/engineio/server.py", line 220, in handle_request
        environ, start_response)
      File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/engineio/socket.py", line 77, in handle_get_request
        raise
    TypeError: exceptions must be old-style classes or derived from BaseException, not NoneType

Everything keeps running though so it's not a major issue. I've looked into it on my side and it looks like when you try to re-raise the IOError in handle_get_request Python has lost the reference to that exception.

This change seems to fix the problem:

    index 240ea43..268313d 100755
    --- a/engineio/socket.py
    +++ b/engineio/socket.py
    @@ -74,9 +74,9 @@ class Socket(object):
                                                               start_response)
             try:
                 packets = self.poll()
    -        except IOError:
    +        except IOError as e:
                 self.close(wait=False)
    -            raise
    +            raise e
             return packets
     
         def handle_post_request(self, environ):

It's a simple enough change, but let me know if you would prefer a pull request.

issue_title: Best practice: Emitting on ZMQ event received in separate thread
issue_body:
Hey everyone,

this is rather a general question than an issue...
I'm getting started in Python and Flask / Flask-SocketIO, so please excuse me, if the question or my understanding are lacking basic things... (If possible, just point me in the right direction).

I'm working on the following application and so far, I've seen a multitude of differnt options on how to implement it and between changes between versions, I'm about to get (very) lost..
I'm not specifically looking for code, I'm more interested in the idea I should pursue and why...

At the core of my system is a Raspberry Pi with Tinkerforge modules (https://www.tinkerforge.com) attached to record humidity and various other sensors. I'm obtaining measurements from these modules at a fixed rate of every second. Then these measurements are saved in a sqlite3 db and published via a ZeroMQ (http://zeromq.org/) publisher.
Works like a charm !! I can even access the values, published via ZMQ, from LabVIEW !!

Now comes the part that gives me trouble...
I would like to present the recorded values in some kind of live-dashboard...
Naturally the idea would be to also run the necessary server on the RPi and only connect with some frontend, if interested in the values... And having worked alittle with NodeJS before, I started looking at SocketIO for on the fly updates... (This way I could also later run the server on a more powerful system, if the RPi is too weak or otherwise occupied doing more important stuff.. That's why I kept the recording/saving/publishing separate from the visual presentation)
I got the system to work smoothly, for what I have in mind as follows:
Separately from the Python program, that records the measurements and publishes as ZeroMQ messages, I wrote a Flask-SocketIO server (NO eventlet / gevent installed)..
This program spawns a separate thread (import threading), that connects to the ZMQ publisher and waits to receive a message from the separate code via ZMQ Publish.
When a ZMQ message is received, the SocketIO emits with a custom event (correct term?).
This custom event is received from the SocketIO.js in the webpage and processed, added to a graph and the graphs on the page are updates (graphs with Highcharts.js)
Perfect, but ...
Looking at the log outputs, I saw a lot of "polling" mentioned and started to look into that further...
After installing eventlet nad changing the code to use eventlet, even if Debug = True, I got the page to load, but no SocketIO updates... until I inserted a "time.sleep" in the emitting function... (not necessary before with no eventlet...).
In my reverse-engineering understanding, this is necessary for the threading to work properly and have the main thread running the Flask-SocketIO server being able to actually emit what was given to it by the separate thread to emit.. Why doesn't this happen when using Werkzeug? (Because of polling?)

In my understanding, this leaves me with a forced sleep there to run via eventlet, that is not necessary when using the Flask development server Werkzeug... I don't like "Magic Delay Fairies".. But I get the feeling that this is due to me not understanding something properly...

Based on the description of my system above, what is the favorable way to implement the ZMQ event-driven emission of SocketIO "messages"?

Please find my code attached (Directly incorporating screwed up the Markdown and attaching py-files didn't work either, so they became text files..)

Cheers
Niels Göran

system_core - Record data, save to DB and ZMQ publish
[system_core.txt](https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/python-socketio/files/723980/system_core.txt)

server - ZMQ event-driven emission of SocketIO
[server.txt](https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/python-socketio/files/723986/server.txt)

issue_title: Discussion and troubleshooting on PyPI (pip) installation with Windows (the newest 0.9.5 release)
issue_body:
The PyPI mxnet installation is now available with windows:

- [mxnet-cu80-win](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet-cu80-win/0.9.5) on Windows, supports CUDA-8.0 and cuDNN-6.0
- the cpu only is to be later

Install:

1. install  [NVIDIA Display Driver](http://www.nvidia.com/Download/index.aspx) Newest
2. install  [CUDA](https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda-downloads)
3. install  [Anaconda3 ](https://www.continuum.io/downloads) (Highly Recommended)  or standard [Python](https://www.python.org/)
4. pip install mxnet-cu80-win

Is currently in test. welcome feedback

## Steps to reproduce
I write my own VOCDetection, here is the code:

```
"""Pascal VOC object detection dataset."""
from __future__ import absolute_import
from __future__ import division
import os
import logging
import numpy as np
try:
    import xml.etree.cElementTree as ET
except ImportError:
    import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
import mxnet as mx

import os
from mxnet.gluon.data import dataset

class ClassProperty(object):
    """Readonly @ClassProperty descriptor for internal usage."""
    def __init__(self, fget):
        self.fget = fget

    def __get__(self, owner_self, owner_cls):
        return self.fget(owner_cls)


class VisionDataset(dataset.Dataset):
    """Base Dataset with directory checker.
    Parameters
    ----------
    root : str
        The root path of xxx.names, by defaut is '~/.mxnet/datasets/foo', where
        `foo` is the name of the dataset.
    """
    def __init__(self, root):
        if not os.path.isdir(os.path.expanduser(root)):
            helper_msg = "{} is not a valid dir. Did you forget to initalize \
                         datasets described in: \
                         `http://gluon-cv.mxnet.io/build/examples_datasets/index.html`? \
                         You need to initialize each dataset only once.".format(root)
            raise OSError(helper_msg)

    @property
    def classes(self):
        raise NotImplementedError

    @property
    def num_class(self):
        """Number of categories."""
        return len(self.classes)


class VOCDetection(VisionDataset):
    """Pascal VOC detection Dataset.
    Parameters
    ----------
    root : str, default '~/mxnet/datasets/voc'
        Path to folder storing the dataset.
    splits : list of tuples, default ((2007, 'trainval'), (2012, 'trainval'))
        List of combinations of (year, name)
        For years, candidates can be: 2007, 2012.
        For names, candidates can be: 'train', 'val', 'trainval', 'test'.
    transform : callable, defaut None
        A function that takes data and label and transforms them. Refer to
        :doc:`./transforms` for examples.
        A transform function for object detection should take label into consideration,
        because any geometric modification will require label to be modified.
    index_map : dict, default None
        In default, the 20 classes are mapped into indices from 0 to 19. We can
        customize it by providing a str to int dict specifying how to map class
        names to indicies. Use by advanced users only, when you want to swap the orders
        of class labels.
    preload_label : bool, default True
        If True, then parse and load all labels into memory during
        initialization. It often accelerate speed but require more memory
        usage. Typical preloaded labels took tens of MB. You only need to disable it
        when your dataset is extreamly large.
    """
    CLASSES = ("shuxiangliefeng","xiexiangliefeng","jiefengposui","liefeng","banjiaoboluo",
                "xiaobuding","hengxiangliefeng","shousuoliefeng","qipi","zongxiangliefeng",
                "kengdong","qianfengliaosun","qipiguilie","zhongxiangliefeng","jiaochaliefeng",
                "banjiaotuoluo","jiaoyuduanlie","zhongxianghengxiangliefeng")
    def __init__(self, root=os.path.join('~', '.mxnet', 'datasets', 'voc'),
                 splits=((2007, 'trainval'), (2012, 'trainval')),
                 transform=None, index_map=None, preload_label=True):
        super(VOCDetection, self).__init__(root)
        self._im_shapes = {}
        self._root = os.path.expanduser(root)
        self._transform = transform
        self._splits = splits
        self._items = self._load_items(splits)
        self._anno_path = os.path.join('{}', 'Annotations', '{}.xml')
        self._image_path = os.path.join('{}', 'JPEGImages', '{}.png')
        self.index_map = index_map or dict(zip(self.classes, range(self.num_class)))
        self._label_cache = self._preload_labels() if preload_label else None

    def __str__(self):
        detail = ','.join([str(s[0]) + s[1] for s in self._splits])
        return self.__class__.__name__ + '(' + detail + ')'

    @property
    def classes(self):
        """Category names."""
        return type(self).CLASSES

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self._items)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        img_id = self._items[idx]
        img_path = self._image_path.format(*img_id)
        label = self._label_cache[idx] if self._label_cache else self._load_label(idx)
        print(img_path)
        img = mx.image.imread(img_path, 1)
        if self._transform is not None:
            return self._transform(img, label)
        print(label, img.shape)
        return img, label

    def _load_items(self, splits):
        """Load individual image indices from splits."""
        ids = []
        for year, name in splits:
            root = os.path.join(self._root, 'VOC' + str(year))
            lf = os.path.join(root, 'ImageSets', 'Main', name + '.txt')
            with open(lf, 'r') as f:
                ids += [(root, line.strip()) for line in f.readlines()]
        return ids

    def _load_label(self, idx):
        """Parse xml file and return labels."""
        img_id = self._items[idx]
        anno_path = self._anno_path.format(*img_id)
        root = ET.parse(anno_path).getroot()
        size = root.find('size')
        width = float(size.find('width').text)
        height = float(size.find('height').text)
        if idx not in self._im_shapes:
            # store the shapes for later usage
            self._im_shapes[idx] = (width, height)
        label = []
        for obj in root.iter('object'):
            difficult = int(obj.find('difficult').text)
            cls_name = obj.find('name').text.strip().lower()
            if cls_name not in self.classes:
                continue
            cls_id = self.index_map[cls_name]
            xml_box = obj.find('bndbox')
            xmin = (float(xml_box.find('xmin').text) - 1)
            ymin = (float(xml_box.find('ymin').text) - 1)
            xmax = (float(xml_box.find('xmax').text) - 1)
            ymax = (float(xml_box.find('ymax').text) - 1)
            try:
                self._validate_label(xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax, width, height)
            except AssertionError as e:
                raise RuntimeError("Invalid label at {}, {}".format(anno_path, e))
            label.append([xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax, cls_id, difficult])
        return np.array(label)

    def _validate_label(self, xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax, width, height):
        """Validate labels."""
        assert xmin >= 0 and xmin < width, (
            "xmin must in [0, {}), given {}".format(width, xmin))
        assert ymin >= 0 and ymin < height, (
            "ymin must in [0, {}), given {}".format(height, ymin))
        assert xmax > xmin and xmax <= width, (
            "xmax must in (xmin, {}], given {}".format(width, xmax))
        assert ymax > ymin and ymax <= height, (
            "ymax must in (ymin, {}], given {}".format(height, ymax))

    def _preload_labels(self):
        """Preload all labels into memory."""
        logging.debug("Preloading %s labels into memory...", str(self))
        return [self._load_label(idx) for idx in range(len(self))]

```
and I replace the VOCDetection in `train_ssd.py`.

## What have you tried to solve it?

1. I have add some display info to check my VOCDetection, It should be ok to produce the batch images and merge labels(bbox\cls label\...).
2. I add display info in `dataloader.py` ， and it seem the `self._data_buffer` is a empty dict and when run the `idx, batch = self._data_queue.get()`， It will be hold on and never restore to run.

issue_title: Cannot deduplicate methods that use forced kwargs
issue_body:
When using python3's syntax for forced kwargs notation, it is impossible to deduplicate:

```python
@asynq.tools.deduplicate()
def foo(pos, *, forcenamed):
    print(pos, forcenamed)
```

The error

```
File "/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/asynq/tools.py", line 254, in decorator
    argspec = getargspec(original_fn)
File "qcore/inspection.py", line 133, in qcore.inspection.getargspec (qcore/inspection.c:3949)
    File "qcore/inspection.py", line 149, in qcore.inspection.getargspec (qcore/inspection.c:3696)
ValueError: keyword-only arguments are not supported by getargspec()
```

will be thrown.

I believe `getfullargspec` needs to be used instead for these. I'm not sure if python2 has `getfullargspec`.

issue_title: strlen may slow
Dashes in .data() names behavior change
issue_body:
We're using camelCase for data names where possible, and only use the dash-name if it exists in the object already. Ideally, API callers should use camelCase for accessing data names rather than dashes. If the caller gets the raw data object they'll see camelCase if they put in dash-name via the API. (Clearly this will need a longer explanation somewhere.)

https://github.com/jquery/jquery/issues/1751
https://github.com/jquery/jquery/issues/2257

issue_title: expand_event limits events instead of expanding them
issue_body:
passing an event without recurrences to expand_events should not affect the event.

currently a call to expand_events removes an event if it starts after the limit provided to expand_events.

it is ok, that occurrences of an event after the limit are not returned (for performance reasons) but plain events should survive.
usecase: event-index of a portal. we limit recurrences to today+365days, but want to show events happening next year too.

```python
>>> from plone.app.event.base import RET_MODE_OBJECTS
>>> from plone.app.event.base import expand_events
>>> from datetime import timedelta
>>> from datetime import datetime

>>> event.start_date
datetime.datetime(2018, 8, 11, 15, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Vienna' CEST+2:00:00 DST>)

no end-given - event is returned
>>> expand_events([event],RET_MODE_OBJECTS,start=datetime.now())
[<Event at /Plone/testevent>]

limiting occurences, also removes the plain event:
>>> expand_events([event],RET_MODE_OBJECTS,start=datetime.now(), end=datetime.now()+timedelta(365))
[]
```

issue_title: Inconsistencies of MapReduce when using http or pbc
issue_body:
I have the following Erlang code (very simple, just for illustration)

```erlang
-module(grid_mr).

-export([yearfun/3]).

yearfun(O, _KeyData, _Arg) ->
  {struct, Map} = mochijson2:decode(riak_object:get_value(O)),
  Year = proplists:get_value(<<"year">>, Map, -1.0),
  Grid = proplists:get_value(<<"grid">>, Map, -1.0),
  case Year > 2006 of
     true -> [{Grid, Year}];
     false -> []
end.
```

When I run a MR code via CURL, I get

```shell
$ curl -XPOST http://172.17.12.21:8089/mapred \
   -H 'Content-Type: application/json'   \
   -d '{"inputs":[["STATS", grid_stats_C7FF2796D6BD1153E847E84277F6B4A1022E29DACA35989004C10FFA92E2A5F0-2008"],
["STATS",  grid_stats_C7FF2796D6BD1153E847E84277F6B4A1022E29DACA35989004C10FFA92E2A5F0-2010"]],"query":[{"map":{"language":"erlang","module":"grid_mr","function":"yearfun"}}]}'

{"C7FF2796D6BD1153E847E84277F6B4A1022E29DACA35989004C10FFA92E2A5F0":2008,"C7FF2796D6BD1153E847E84277F6B4A1022E29DACA35989004C10FFA92E2A5F0":2010}
```

However when submitting with Python using http

```python
from riak import RiakClient
from riak import RiakMapReduce
riak = RiakClient(protocol='http', host='172.17.12.22', http_port=8089)
bucket = riak.bucket("STATS")
mr = RiakMapReduce(riak)
keys = ["grid_stats_C7FF2796D6BD1153E847E84277F6B4A1022E29DACA35989004C10FFA92E2A5F0-2008", 
        "grid_stats_C7FF2796D6BD1153E847E84277F6B4A1022E29DACA35989004C10FFA92E2A5F0-2010"]
mr.add("STATS", keys)
#mr.search(index="grid_stats", query="industry_id:22 AND customer_segmentation_id:6")
mr.map(['grid_mr', 'yearfun'])
for result in mr.run():
    print "%s" % result 
```

I get

```python
C7FF2796D6BD1153E847E84277F6B4A1022E29DACA35989004C10FFA92E2A5F0
```

If, instead, I use `pbc`

```python
from riak import RiakClient
from riak import RiakMapReduce
riak = RiakClient(protocol='pbc', host='172.17.12.22', http_port=8087)
bucket = riak.bucket("STATS")
mr = RiakMapReduce(riak)
keys = ["grid_stats_C7FF2796D6BD1153E847E84277F6B4A1022E29DACA35989004C10FFA92E2A5F0-2008", 
        "grid_stats_C7FF2796D6BD1153E847E84277F6B4A1022E29DACA35989004C10FFA92E2A5F0-2010"]
mr.add("STATS", keys)
#mr.search(index="grid_stats", query="industry_id:22 AND customer_segmentation_id:6")
mr.map(['grid_mr', 'yearfun'])
for result in mr.run():
    print "%s" % result
```
the client raises an exception

```python
[...]
.virtualenvs/numpy/lib/python2.7/site-packages/riak/transports/pbc/transport.pyc in mapred(self, inputs, query, timeout)
    410         for phase, content in self.stream_mapred(inputs, query, timeout):
    411             if phase in result:
--> 412                 result[phase] += content
    413             else:
    414                 result[phase] = content
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +=: 'dict' and 'dict'
```

Am I doing something wrong, or…? Right now the fix seems to change the Erlang code to

```erlang
-module(binary_grid).

-export([yearfun/3]).

yearfun(O, _KeyData, _Arg) ->
  {struct, Map} = mochijson2:decode(riak_object:get_value(O)),
  Year = proplists:get_value(<<"year">>, Map, -1.0),
  Grid = proplists:get_value(<<"grid">>, Map, -1.0),
  case Year > 2006 of
     true -> [list_to_binary(mochijson2:encode([{Grid, Year}]))];
     false -> []
end.
```

Mickaël.

issue_title: Insert only specified columns in `contrib.sqla`
issue_body:
At the moment, `contrib.sqla` tries to insert all columns found in the target table.

By changing two lines in `sqla.run()` and one line in `sqla.copy()`, it would only insert columns that are specified in the `columns` parameter. It would be helpful if the table already exists and we just want to insert a subset of columns.

The lines are: creating `ins_rows` in `run()`, and creating `bound_cols` in `copy()`.

issue_title: 'async' is a reserved keyword in Python 3.7
issue_body:
Basically, `async` is now a reserved keyword in Python 3.7, similar to `return` or `def` in earlier versions. The `duo_client` library makes use of `async` as a keyword argument in a few locations, namely `duo_client/auth.py` and `duo_client/auth_v1.py`, which causes a `SyntaxError` on Python 3.7:

```
ImportError: Failed to import test module: tests.test_client
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/travis/virtualenv/python3.7-dev/lib/python3.7/site-packages/nose2/plugins/loader/discovery.py", line 201, in _find_tests_in_file
    module = util.module_from_name(module_name)
  File "/home/travis/virtualenv/python3.7-dev/lib/python3.7/site-packages/nose2/util.py", line 77, in module_from_name
    __import__(name)
  File "/home/travis/build/duosecurity/duo_client_python/tests/test_client.py", line 4, in <module>
    import duo_client.client
  File "/home/travis/build/duosecurity/duo_client_python/duo_client/__init__.py", line 4, in <module>
    from .auth import Auth
  File "/home/travis/build/duosecurity/duo_client_python/duo_client/auth.py", line 115
    async=False,
        ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax
```

TravisCI is catching this as well: https://travis-ci.org/duosecurity/duo_client_python/jobs/336651090

Python 3.7 is expected to come out sometime in the middle of 2018 (https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0537/#schedule), we should probably fix this error before then. It's worth noting that this change will most likely require breaking changes, and for consumers to update their code to use the new keyword argument.

PEP 8 recommends appending a `_` to make the keyword argument `async_`: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#function-and-method-arguments

issue_body:
In https://github.com/robotframework/Selenium2Library/blob/master/src/Selenium2Library/__init__.py, this is now: 
```
    def _current_browser(self):
        warnings.warn('"Selenium2Library._current_browser" is deprecated, '

    def _current_browser(self):
        warnings.warn('"Selenium2Library._current_browser" is deprecated, '
                      'use "Selenium2Library.browser" instead.',

        return self._browser
```

issue_title: Add `pipenv add PACKAGE` sub command which replace `pipenv install PACKAGE`
issue_labels: [{'id': 917288897, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5MTcyODg4OTc=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pypa/pipenv/labels/api%20change', 'name': 'api change', 'color': 'e8776f', 'default': False}, {'id': 729134113, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3MjkxMzQxMTM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pypa/pipenv/labels/discussion', 'name': 'discussion', 'color': '9992e8', 'default': False}, {'id': 723122038, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3MjMxMjIwMzg=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pypa/pipenv/labels/future', 'name': 'future', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}, {'id': 726682597, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3MjY2ODI1OTc=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pypa/pipenv/labels/requires%20bdfl%20approval', 'name': 'requires bdfl approval', 'color': 'b6ed12', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**  
`pipenv install` has two responsibilities:

- Install all packages in `[packages]` (bare `pipenv install`)
- Install specified package and update `Pipfile` and `Pipfile.lock`  (`pipenv install PACKAGE`)

This make confused beginners.

**Describe the solution you'd like**  
I suggest to add `pipenv add PACKAGE` which is equivalent to `pip install PACKAGE`
and to make `pipenv install PACKAGE` deprecated.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**  
Just remove `pipenv install PACKAGE`.

We are able to edit Pipfile If we want to add package.

**Additional context**  

- `yarn` provides `yarn add PACKAGE` correspond to `pipenv install PACKAGE`. `yarn install PACKAGE` was removed.
- `bundler` does not provide sub command correspond to `pipenv install PACKAGE`

issue_title: Pipfile.lock should use flat collection of dependencies
issue_body:
Currently, `Pipfile.lock` puts groups at top-level, then lists out locked dependencies for each group separately.

This is not, for example, how npm or Yarn handle this. Both of those package managers have a flat list of locked packages, with `dev` as a flag for each package.

The latter setup is superior, as it would make it structurally impossible to have version conflicts in the lockfile per https://github.com/kennethreitz/pipenv/issues/1137.

As such, `Pipfile.lock` should use a flat collection of dependencies, as by construction it should not be possible for locked default and development to conflict, as that would give rise to inconsistencies and unsatisfied transitive dependencies.

issue_title: fput_object: AWS S3 multipart upload fails
issue_body: minio version: 4.0.2 ## Reproduce Steps The bug happened on our system, and some minimal steps would be this (not tested, sorry): ```python from minio import Minio minio_client = Minio(...) # setup to run against AWS S3 amz_meta_data = {'x_amz_meta-sha256': 'foo'} minio_client.fput_object(bucket_name='our-bucket-name', object_name='large_file.jpeg', file_path='/path/to/large_file.jpeg', content_type='image/jpeg', metadata=amz_meta_data) ``` ## Current Behaviour An `InvalidArgument: InvalidArgument: message: Invalid Argument` exception is thrown, the upload is not finished. ## Possible Solution I debugged this, and what happens is apparently that an AWS S3 multipart upload is initiated, which consists of (among other things) a [Initiate Multipart Upload](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/mpUploadInitiate.html) and an [Upload Part](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/mpUploadUploadPart.html) request. The initiate multipart request runs through, but the upload part request has a 400 error response: ``` InvalidArgumentMetadata cannot be specified in this context.x-amz-meta-sha2561f/29/1f29c29fb3fde0d6f9a211d74872c1b0b267d09e83e6a18bfb7b1a4d71c0352e ``` Some googling found [this answer on the AWS forum](https://forums.aws.amazon.com/thread.jspa?threadID=223994), where it is pointed out that you shouldn't pass metadata to the upload part request, only to the initiate multipart upload request. Therefore, I suspect the solution is to adjust the minio code accordingly.

issue_title: message.value is None, stability and other possible problems
issue_body:
Hi, 

These are some problems that we meet using pykafka consumer. Even though, there are some other tools in the chain, I have a feeling that the reason is in pykafka (using the 2.1.2 release). No proofs, unfortunately, this is just for the record. 

### m.value is None
Sometimes, when I do  m= message.cosume(), the m.value is None, while None is never posted to kafka. 
Related thing: we have several consumers (with different consumer group) in parallel on the topic. One started to get None values (because it tried to parse the m.value), the other forwarded the messages (no matter its content) via bruce to another kafka, and the (pykafka) consumer there did not say anything about None messages (at least, it seems so). 

### Failed to acquire partition
We have two consumers running from the one consumer group, treating each two partitions 

One of them got  stuck for some reasons, and the other could not acquire partitions for ~12 hours, 
the code was trying to recreate consumer with 1 seconds delay, but every time got this error:
```
16-01-17 02:45:20,633 - pykafka.balancedconsumer - WARNING - Failed to acquire partition <pykafka.partition.Partition at 0x7f1dd195abd0 (id=2)> after 4 retries.
2016-01-17 02:45:20,634 - pykafka.balancedconsumer - ERROR - Stopping consumer in response to error
2016-01-17 02:45:20,642 - kafkarecon.kafkarecon - ERROR - Got an exception! ConsumerStoppedException()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/kafka2aero/kafkarecon/kafkarecon.py", line 98, in start
    m = self.consumer.consume()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py", line 694, in consume
    raise ConsumerStoppedException
ConsumerStoppedException
```
thus, downtime for 12h.

### repeated message reception

We have such a dataflow   php -> bruce (kafka producer daemon) -> kafka -> pykafka balacned consumer -> bruce -> kafka (another one) -> pykafka balanced consumer -> mysql. 

php puts json messages with unique values in field id.  Never None (see the first issue). So, at the end, last pykafka consumer receives (rarely, but it does) repeated messages, thus, getting duplicate on primary key exception with mysql.  I logged the values, so it's really repeated message   kafka->mysql script tries to put in mysql. Here you could blame any of the tools (and arrows) in the chain, but I think it can be related to pykafka.


Any enlightenments or guesses are welcome

Thanks

issue_title: Running `rapidfire` in Python results in multiply nested directories
issue_body: It isn't obvious to me from the spec that this is intended. I would expect that all directories would be at the same level as if one had run `launch_rocket` multiple times. Instead, it appears that the directories become deeply nested. This seems to be caused by multiple calls to `os.chdir`. If this is indeed intended and expected behavior, I would expect at the minimum that we would change back to the original directory after a run.

issue_title: For diff-based checks provide "expected" and "actual" attributes
issue_body: ``assertEquals`` and other failures should provide ``actual`` and ``expected`` attributes so Intellij may display ``<Click here to see the difference>``

issue_title: "pycapnp==0.5.5" is not in `bindings/py/requirements.txt`
issue_body:
README.md states:

> The Python dependencies (NumPy and pycapnp) can be installed with pip:
> 
> ```
> pip install -r bindings/py/requirements.txt
> ```

But capnp is not indeed in `bindings/py/requirements.txt`, instead, it's installed by `extra_require` in `setup.py`:

```python
  setup(
    name="nupic.bindings",
    version=getVersion(),
    # This distribution contains platform-specific C++ libraries, but they are not
    # built with distutils. So we must create a dummy Extension object so when we
    # create a binary file it knows to make it platform-specific.
    ext_modules=[Extension('nupic.dummy', sources = ['dummy.c'])], 
    namespace_packages=["nupic"],
    install_requires=findRequirements(platform),
    packages=find_packages(),
    package_data={
        "nupic.proto": ["*.capnp"],
        "nupic.bindings": ["*.so", "*.pyd"],
    },
    extras_require = {"capnp": ["pycapnp==0.5.5"]},
```

Is this intended? Why?

This causes the following 2 problems:

* A new user would fail if only goes "Building from Source -> Prerequisites" to "Developer Installation -> Configure and generate C++ build files" on a whole new environment
* inconsistency between documentation and actual code

issue_title: pageSize attribute of <template> tag
issue_body:
Hey,

I spent hours trying switch to "a4 landscape" page using z3c.rml and I finally found that IPageTemplate contains "pagesize" attribute definition instead of "pageSize". Take a look at https://github.com/zopefoundation/z3c.rml/blob/master/src/z3c/rml/template.py#L161

Ok, changing attr name fixes my problem, but:
  * most of (?) attribute names are created with camel case notation
  * examples of RML (from ReportLab) contains pageSize attribute (http://www.reportlab.com/examples/rml/test/test_007_forms.rml)
  * RML DTD contains pageSize attribute (http://www.reportlab.com/docs/rml.dtd)

The `-x/--stop-on-error` flag has as the description "stop on first error or failure". When a test suite has multiple layers and this flag is passed the test suite will stop running tests **for that layer**, but not for all layers, which is what I expect, given that description.

Is this feature imprecisely described?

issue_title: Add ability to draw from arbitrary distributions in RandomWalk profile
issue_body:
Currently we take the limit `nsep -> infinity` in order to draw from a Gaussian rather than actually doing a step-by-step random walk.  This is a very good approximation, even for low number of steps.

Consider extending this to allow drawing from other distributions, the most natural being other surface brightness profiles.

issue_title: [gtk3] Prefs window doesn't pop up nicely
issue_body:
The way the Preferences window appears isn't too nice.

It's hard to tell exactly what happens because it happens so quickly, but it resizes and moves itself at least once (maybe twice) after being presented (with some/all of its contents being transparent at the first size and location) until it finally settles and shows its stuff.

Ideally, whenever it appears, it should appear at its final size and position. This probably requires that all its contents (including the dynamically generated ones, such as the actual keyboard shortcuts, the possible choices in combo boxes) are filled in before presenting the window.

JIRAError does not correctly display full error information
issue_body:
There seems to be a bug in `JIRAError.__str__`

        if self.response and hasattr(self.response, 'text'):
            t += "\n\tresponse text = " % self.response.headers

https://github.com/pycontribs/jira/blob/master/jira/utils.py#L161

1. This checks for the `text` attribute but then uses the `headers` attribute (cut-and-paste error from the lines above?)
2. The response object is "Truthy" based upon the HTTP response, so in the case of a HTTP error it is actually "False" so the conditional check fails.
3. Which is just as well, because once you fix this you'll see that the format string is invalid and causes an exception because it is missing a %s at the end

:-)

I'll submit a pull request to fix this up shortly.

issue_title: Change how Lists and NumpyArrays are handled at the command line
issue_body:
Currently, the way that lists and arrays are handled at the command line is to set nargs to *, allowing 1 or more arguments for the variable at the command line. If we had a schema with an item mylist which was a list of ints, we would invoke it like so at the command line:

    example_program --mylist 1 2 3 4 5 6

This handling is nice and pretty simple, and allows (requires) spaces between elements of the argument. The downside is that for any more complicated arrangement of lists or arrays (for example a list of lists or a multidimensional array), it is impossible to set them at the command line. For example, if I had a schema with a list of lists of ints, all of the following attempts at invoking result in validation errors because the elements are not lists:

    example_program --mylist 1 2 3 4 5 6
    example_program --mylist [1 2 3] [4 5 6]
    example_program --mylist [[1 2 3] [4 5 6]]
    example_program --mylist [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]]

I'd like to propose using ast.literal eval for handling lists and arrays in the argument parser. This would result in setting arrays and lists at the command line like the last of the above examples.

issue_title: enhancements to the pathos.profile interface
issue_body:
Some suggested improvements to `pathos.profile` (made by @rkatriel-mdsol):

1. Assuming this is possible, it might be less confusing for new users if the pool used by the pipe was encapsulated in `profile()`. Perhaps it could be a default value for the argument.
2. Each thread prints out separate profiling output, even though they call the same functions (with different data). An option to accumulate across all threads would be very nice.

issue_title: Profile `./pants list` overhead with the daemon
issue_body:
For the completely warm case with the daemon, very little time is spent in the engine (since all products have already been computed) and the daemon, and this is confirmed by the daemon's log. But a lot of time is currently unaccounted-for, and it would be good to have an understanding of where the time is going (in case it is something architecturally important).

As an example, the daemon's log for a warm run of `./pants list 3rdparty::` in Twitter's source repo looks like:

```
I1005 13:36:11.372629 9950 pailgun_server.py:72] handling pailgun request: `./pants list 3rdparty::`
D1005 13:36:11.373440 9950 pailgun_server.py:73] pailgun request environment: <snip!>
D1005 13:36:11.374396 9950 pailgun_service.py:58] execution commandline: [u'./pants', u'list', u'3rdparty::']
D1005 13:36:11.421937 9950 git.py:292] Executing: git --git-dir=/Users/stuhood/src/source/.git --work-tree=/Users/stuhood/src/source merge-base origin/master HEAD
D1005 13:36:11.531510 9950 git.py:292] Executing: git --git-dir=/Users/stuhood/src/source/.git --work-tree=/Users/stuhood/src/source merge-base origin/master HEAD
D1005 13:36:11.639550 9950 target_roots.py:49] args are: [u'./pants', u'list', u'3rdparty::']
D1005 13:36:11.639993 9950 target_roots.py:50] spec_roots are: [DescendantAddresses(directory=u'3rdparty')]
D1005 13:36:11.640206 9950 target_roots.py:51] changed_request is: ChangedRequest(changes_since=None, diffspec=None, include_dependees='none', fast=False)
D1005 13:36:11.640548 9950 pailgun_service.py:67] warming the product graph via <pants.pantsd.service.scheduler_service.SchedulerService object at 0x10296f510>
D1005 13:36:11.641108 9950 engine_initializer.py:70] warming target_roots for: LiteralTargetRoots([DescendantAddresses(directory=u'3rdparty')])
D1005 13:36:11.641623 9950 scheduler.py:643] ran 0 scheduling iterations, 0 runnables, and 0 steps in 0.000011 seconds. there are 27592 total nodes.
D1005 13:36:11.642249 9950 process_manager.py:194] purging metadata directory: /Users/stuhood/src/source/.pids/pantsd-run-2016-10-05T13_36_11_641971
D1005 13:36:11.642498 9950 process_manager.py:425] forking <pants.bin.daemon_pants_runner.DaemonPantsRunner object at 0x105726390>
```

The log (assuming we can trust it) indicates that we are spending a total of `0.270` milliseconds in the daemon (`642-372`) and `0.000011` milliseconds in the engine.

But on the frontend for the same run, there is a lot of time un-accounted for:

```
$ time ./pants -ldebug list 3rdparty:: | wc -l
<snip>
    1660

real    0m2.526s
user    0m0.283s
sys 0m0.185s
```

---

One issue is obvious in the frontend with the `-ldebug` flag: some work is being re-executed post-fork (likely due to https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/4168/ cc @kwlzn), but it still only accounts for a fraction of the total runtime.

issue_title: soco.events doesn't handle network disconnects gracefully
issue_body:
After the event_listener has been imported from soco.events, a network disconnect will trigger errors. 

Without rewriting soco.events, I haven't found a way to handle these errors gracefully in my application, not least because soco.events calls unsubscribe at exit (see line 477 in soco.events.py) which will trigger errors at exit if the network has gone down.

I'm planning to fork soco.events so that it uses twisted.internet rather than requests. Then the event server won't need to run in a thread. It's threaded nature seems to be causing other intermittent problems at shutdown.

issue_title: SecurityRule object referencing
issue_body:
As mentioned in #22 and later discussed in greater detail on Gitter, we would like the ability to have direct references to objects that make up a SecurityRule instead of just their names in string form. 

@btorresgil and I agreed this functionality should not change the way the library interprets or stores the config tree. So objects will still be children of the same parents they have always been. We are simply talking about creating references to those objects in the SecurityRule at this time.

This will most likely entail a "link_references" like method that the developer will invoke _after_ the SecurityRule and the objects have been refreshed or otherwise loaded. 

My thinking is that we add additional attributes to the SecurityRule class to not interfere with the present functionality. So for example, `source` will still contain a list of string names, but `source_obj` will be added to store a list of `objects.AddressObject` and `objects.AddressGroup` objects. This way, the read/write functionality of the SecurityRule object is not changed. We could also make it so that when an object is added or removed from the `source_obj` list, the same thing happens to its corresponding string name in `source` string list. 

Obviously the above would apply to all currently represented objects in the `objects` module that have anything to do with a SecurityRule.

issue_title: Connections are not reused
issue_body:
This library [creates a new `Session` for each request](https://github.com/Mangopay/mangopay2-python-sdk/blob/v2/mangopaysdk/tools/resttool.py#L110), which means that each one opens a new HTTP(S) connection, and that's a lot of overhead when making more than a few requests.

Background and details: https://github.com/liberapay/liberapay.com/issues/382.

Pull Request coming soon.

issue_labels: [{'id': 656403030, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ni/nimi-python/labels/API', 'name': 'API', 'color': 'fbca04', 'default': False}, {'id': 742577049, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ni/nimi-python/labels/nidcpower', 'name': 'nidcpower', 'color': '363fa0', 'default': False}, {'id': 780102262, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ni/nimi-python/labels/nidmm', 'name': 'nidmm', 'color': '6633a0', 'default': False}, {'id': 733087933, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ni/nimi-python/labels/nifgen', 'name': 'nifgen', 'color': 'ffcccf', 'default': False}, {'id': 735743004, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ni/nimi-python/labels/niscope', 'name': 'niscope', 'color': '3f5cc6', 'default': False}, {'id': 771497718, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ni/nimi-python/labels/niswitch', 'name': 'niswitch', 'color': '7fe871', 'default': False}, {'id': 763890424, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ni/nimi-python/labels/priority-high', 'name': 'priority-high', 'color': 'c61d94', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Functions such as `nidcpower.Session.configure_digital_edge_measure_trigger(self, input_terminal, edge=enums.DigitalEdge.RISING)` will 90% of the time be called with one argument.

This will set 3 attributes: `measure_trigger_type`, `digital_edge_measure_trigger_input_terminal`, `digital_edge_measure_trigger_edge` (which has a default value in the function).

From [Zen of Python](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0020/#id3):

> There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it.

It may be best to only have one mechanism.

This also applies to other trigger types with similar configure functions such as: `configure_software_edge_measure_trigger`... which at this time is removed from the nidcpower Python API. We should at the very least be consistent in how we approach it.
